title: AML Agents Briefing — Thursday, 28 May 2026
script:
ALEX: Welcome to AML Agents Briefing. It's Thursday the 28th of May 2026, and this is your audio overview of today's most material AML and financial crime developments across the Asia-Pacific region. Full detail, the obligation register, and horizon scanning are all at amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

JORDAN: Big day, Alex. We've got Australia's Scam Prevention Framework moving into live governance, Mastercard clearing a major crypto licensing threshold, and an AI deal-material tool raising some sharp questions for KYC integrity. Let's get into it.

ALEX: Starting with the highest-impact story this morning. Australia's federal government has released designations, draft industry codes, and draft rules under the Scam Prevention Framework, which we'll call the SPF from here. The Australian Banking Association has responded publicly, signalling it's ready to engage. This is the moment the SPF shifts from policy architecture into operational reality for Australian banks.

JORDAN: Alex, what's actually changed for banks today compared to where they were yesterday morning?

ALEX: The release of the designations and draft codes means banks are now named participants with specific obligations taking shape in black and white. The draft rules will set out liability allocation between platforms, telcos, and banks when a customer suffers a scam loss. That liability question is the one that's been keeping heads of fraud and financial crime compliance awake for months.

JORDAN: Right, and the ABA's public signal of engagement matters because it tells you the sector expects to negotiate some of the code detail before it's locked in. So there's still a window.

ALEX: Exactly. The consultation period is the moment to shape your exposure.

JORDAN: So what should listeners do? If you're the head of fraud or the MLRO at an Australian bank, your immediate action is to pull the draft codes and draft rules this week and map them against your existing scam reimbursement policy and your customer remediation framework. Pay close attention to how the liability allocation rules interact with your transaction monitoring alerts for authorised push payment scams. Before your next risk committee meeting, you want a clear read on whether your current controls meet the threshold the draft codes describe, or whether you have a gap that needs remediation funding.

ALEX: Moving on to the Mastercard story, which has implications well beyond Australia. Mastercard Transaction Services has obtained a BitLicense from the New York State Department of Financial Services. For listeners outside the United States, the BitLicense is one of the most demanding virtual-currency regulatory thresholds in the world. Clearing it positions Mastercard to offer stablecoin and tokenised deposit services at scale.

JORDAN: And the read-across for APAC compliance teams is real. If Mastercard is now a licensed virtual asset service provider under a demanding framework, your counterparty risk assessments for payment rails involving Mastercard stablecoin products need to be updated. The correspondent banking due diligence question becomes: what does Mastercard's BitLicense status mean for your own obligations under FATF Recommendation 16 on wire transfers, and under MAS Notice 626 if you're a Singapore-regulated institution? Add a Mastercard stablecoin scenario to your next virtual asset counterparty review.

ALEX: Also on the tokenisation theme, and this is worth flagging for horizon-scanning purposes, the Bank for International Settlements has published results from its Project Agorá prototype. The project demonstrated that tokenising commercial bank deposits alongside central bank money on a unified ledger can materially improve the speed and traceability of wholesale cross-border payments. Multiple central banks and private institutions participated. The compliance implication is that travel rule obligations and transaction monitoring logic built for correspondent banking may need to be rearchitected for tokenised settlement environments.

JORDAN: Compliance architects and technology leads at major banks should add Project Agorá to their next architecture review agenda. The BIS findings are not theoretical at this point.

ALEX: Now, shifting our focus, Jordan, over to you for the third story.

JORDAN: Yes, and this one is less obviously an AML story at first glance, but stick with it. A firm called Farsight has launched an AI agent that can generate client-ready deal materials from a single prompt. The immediate question this raises for compliance is about the integrity of know-your-customer and suitability documentation in institutional financial services. If deal materials, including client-facing representations of counterparty profiles, can be generated by AI at speed, the question is whether your KYC documentation is being reviewed or merely reproduced.

ALEX: And the read-across is to your first line of defence. The risk is that relationship managers use AI-generated outputs to populate KYC files without independent verification of the underlying source data.

JORDAN: Exactly. For MLROs and heads of financial crime compliance, the action here is to add an AI-generated documentation risk scenario to your next KYC quality assurance thematic review. Check whether your KYC policy requires attestation of source data, not just completion of the form. If it doesn't, it needs to.

ALEX: Before we close, what to watch. There are three near-term items listeners should have on their radar. Australian compliance officers should note that AUSTRAC published detailed Tranche 2 guidance for legal practitioners in late April, covering source-of-wealth diligence in conveyancing and SMR submission practicalities. And AUSTRAC's recent 47 million Australian dollar civil penalty against a digital currency exchange for systemic failures in transaction monitoring and SMR timeliness is a live enforcement benchmark for every registered digital currency exchange in Australia right now.

JORDAN: Three concrete steps for the coming week. First, if you're an Australian reporting entity, verify your annual compliance report lodgement status under AML/CTF Rules Part 13. Late lodgement attracts daily civil penalties. Second, for Indonesia-regulated institutions, your CY 2024 annual AML programme review findings should be on their way to your Risk Oversight Committee and Board of Commissioners ahead of the June deadline. Third, Hong Kong-regulated entities should confirm that Internal Audit has validated the CY 2024 self-assessment return before the September submission window closes.

ALEX: Full detail on all of today's stories, the obligation register, and the horizon-scanning feed is at amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

JORDAN: That's your briefing for Thursday the 28th of May. Stay sharp, and we'll see you tomorrow.
